content.text: What Trust Calibration Research Is — and Why It Differs from Usability Testing

Usability research asks whether people can accomplish tasks with a system. Trust calibration research asks whether people rely on a system at an appropriate rate — neither avoiding it when it is correct nor accepting it when it is wrong. These are different questions with different methods. A system can be highly usable and still produce poorly calibrated trust: a clean, confident UI increases automation bias even when the underlying model accuracy does not warrant it.

For AI products, miscalibrated trust in either direction is a launch risk. Under-trust means the feature delivers no value — users redo the AI's work manually, defeating the time-saving rationale. Over-trust means users accept AI errors at a rate that causes downstream harm, which is a liability risk for regulated domains and a quality risk everywhere else. Trust calibration research runs before launch to characterise where a user population sits on the under-trust to over-trust continuum and to design interventions that bring them to an appropriate level.
The Three Trust Zones and Their Research Signatures

attrs.alt: Diagram showing three trust zones: under-trust, calibrated, and over-trust with their symptoms and research methods
attrs.title: Diagram showing three trust zones: under-trust, calibrated, and over-trust with their symptoms and research methods

content.text: Every individual and role cohort sits in one of three zones relative to a specific AI capability. The zones are not fixed — they depend on the task, the model, and the user's experience level. A senior physician may be well-calibrated on diagnostic support AI but over-trusting of scheduling AI because they have more experience in medicine than in operational tools.
Trust Zone
Definition
Behavioural Signatures
Primary Risk
Under-trust
User expects worse accuracy than the AI actually delivers
Always overrides; redoes AI work; never uses autonomous features
Feature delivers no value; low adoption
Calibrated
User expectation matches actual AI accuracy within ~15 percentage points
Accepts AI output at the same rate as AI is correct; verifies high-stakes output; overrides when they detect an error
Minimal — this is the target state
Over-trust
User expects better accuracy than the AI actually delivers
Never overrides; skips verification; defers to AI on tasks outside its training distribution
Errors pass undetected; liability and quality harm

The calibration gap — the signed difference between a participant's accuracy expectation and the AI's actual accuracy — is the primary research metric. A gap of +0.20 means the participant expects the AI to be 20 percentage points more accurate than it is: a clear over-trust indicator. A gap of -0.25 means they expect it to be 25 points worse than it is: under-trust.
Study Design: Combining Survey and Behavioural Methods
Why Surveys Alone Are Insufficient

Self-reported trust does not reliably predict behaviour under actual AI output. Participants who describe themselves as appropriately sceptical of AI frequently miss seeded errors when reviewing AI output in a realistic task. This gap between stated attitude and behaviour is well-documented in human factors research and is particularly pronounced for AI because the output often appears fluent and confident regardless of correctness. Trust calibration studies must include a behavioural component — something the participant actually does with AI output — not only attitudinal questions.

The recommended design pairs a pre-session survey (to establish baseline expectations and prior experience) with a moderated session containing three task types: an accuracy estimation task, a seeded error review task, and an override decision task. Each task measures a different dimension of trust and produces different intervention signals.
Study Plan: Full YAML Specification

# trust-calibration-study-plan.yaml
meta:
  project: "Document Review AI"
  phase: "Pre-launch trust calibration research"
  participant_target: 16
  roles:
    - id: senior_associate
      count: 6
      screening: "reviews ≥ 20 documents/week"
    - id: partner
      count: 4
      screening: "signs off on final review batches"
    - id: paralegal
      count: 6
      screening: "performs initial triage reviews"

sessions:
  - id: expectation_survey
    format: async_survey
    duration_minutes: 15
    timing: "1 week before prototype session"
    instruments:
      - name: TAS-AI
        full_name: "Trust in AI Scale (adapted)"
        items:
          - "I believe AI can accurately identify relevant clauses in legal documents."
          - "I would act on AI output without additional review."
          - "I trust AI more than junior colleagues for routine document tasks."
        scale: "1 (strongly disagree) to 7 (strongly agree)"
      - name: prior_experience_inventory
        items:
          - "How many months have you used AI tools professionally?"
          - "Name the AI tools you use regularly."
          - "Describe a time when AI output surprised you — positively or negatively."

  - id: calibration_probe_session
    format: moderated_1on1
    duration_minutes: 60
    tasks:
      - id: accuracy_estimation
        instruction: "Before I show you the AI's output, estimate: what percentage of the time do you expect it to correctly identify a material adverse change clause?"
        record: participant_estimate
        compare_to: actual_f1_score

      - id: seeded_error_review
        description: "Participant reviews 10 AI-annotated documents. 3 contain deliberate errors."
        errors_injected:
          - type: false_positive
            description: "Standard limitation clause flagged as material"
            severity: medium
          - type: false_negative
            description: "Genuine MAC clause missed entirely"
            severity: high
          - type: hallucinated_reference
            description: "AI cites Section 4.3 which does not exist in this document"
            severity: blocker
        metrics_collected:
          - errors_caught_count
          - time_to_catch_each_error
          - post_review_confidence_rating

      - id: override_decision_task
        instruction: "The AI flagged this clause as high-risk. Do you accept, modify, or override?"
        variants:
          - ai_confidence_shown: false
          - ai_confidence_shown: true
          - ai_reasoning_shown: true
        record: decision_and_time_on_task

  - id: post_session_debrief
    duration_minutes: 15
    questions:
      - "How did the AI's actual accuracy compare to what you expected?"
      - "Were there moments when you trusted the AI more than you should have? Less?"
      - "What information would help you decide when to verify AI output?"
      - "What would need to change for you to reduce your review time on AI-assisted tasks?"

analysis:
  calibration_gap_metric:
    formula: "participant_accuracy_estimate - actual_f1_score"
    interpretation:
      positive: "Over-trust: participant expects better accuracy than AI delivers"
      negative: "Under-trust: participant expects worse accuracy than AI delivers"
      near_zero: "Calibrated: expectation matches actual performance"
    threshold_for_intervention: 0.15  # 15 percentage points

  automation_bias_score:
    formula: "errors_missed / total_errors_injected"
    per_error_type: true
    target: "< 0.20 for blocker-severity errors"
The Seeded Error Method
Why Deliberate Errors Are the Core Instrument

The seeded error task is the most informative instrument in trust calibration research because it directly measures the behavioural consequence of miscalibrated trust: an error the AI made that the user failed to catch. Surveys and interviews can only capture what participants say they would do; seeded error tasks measure what they actually do when confronted with real AI output containing realistic mistakes.

Designing good seeded errors requires understanding which error types are plausible given the model's actual failure modes. A hallucinated reference (citing a clause that does not exist) is a different failure mode from a misclassification (flagging a standard limitation as material). Both are worth testing, but they surface in different parts of the user's review process and respond to different UX interventions. Work with the model team to identify the top three to five failure modes before designing the error bank.
False positive: AI flags something as significant that is routine — tests whether users verify before escalating
False negative: AI misses something genuinely important — tests whether users have a fallback verification habit
Hallucinated reference: AI cites something that does not exist — tests whether users cross-reference AI citations
Confidence-miscalibrated: AI expresses high confidence on an incorrect output — tests automation bias under confident UI signals
Out-of-distribution: AI receives input outside its training scope and produces plausible-looking nonsense — tests whether users recognise scope limits
Seeded Error Severity Classification

Not all missed errors are equally consequential. A missed false positive costs a user time (they investigate something that was not a problem) but does not create harm. A missed false negative — especially for a blocker-severity error type — means a genuine problem passed undetected. Weight the session design toward higher-severity error types and use severity-stratified miss rates as the primary launch readiness metric.
Error Severity
Definition
Miss Rate Threshold
Intervention Trigger
Blocker
Undetected error causes direct harm: safety, regulatory, financial
> 10% miss rate: launch blocked
Mandatory verification step in UI; alert when AI confidence is low
High
Undetected error causes significant rework or reputational risk
> 25% miss rate: launch requires mitigation
Friction added to accept action; confidence interval displayed
Medium
Undetected error causes minor quality issue correctable in downstream review
> 50% miss rate: monitor post-launch
Accuracy disclaimer in onboarding; sampling audit recommended
Low
Undetected error has no material consequence
No threshold; track for model improvement
No intervention required
Python Implementation: Calibration Analysis

This module loads study results from a directory of per-participant JSON files, computes calibration gaps and automation bias scores by role cohort, and generates an intervention plan that maps directly to onboarding design and UI requirements.
Python Implementation: Seeded Error Session Runner

In a full research system, the session runner coordinates the review UI with the error injection logic, captures interaction timing, and writes structured results. This implementation shows the data model and metric computation; the UI integration is environment-specific.

import json
import random
import time
from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum


class ErrorSeverity(Enum):
    BLOCKER = "blocker"
    HIGH = "high"
    MEDIUM = "medium"
    LOW = "low"


@dataclass
class SeededError:
    document_id: str
    error_type: str  # false_positive | false_negative | hallucination
    severity: ErrorSeverity
    description: str
    ai_annotation: dict  # what the AI showed
    ground_truth: dict   # what is actually correct


class SeededErrorSession:
    # Runs a calibration session with deliberate AI errors injected.
    # Records which errors the participant catches and time taken.

    def __init__(self, participant_id: str, errors: list[SeededError]):
        self.participant_id = participant_id
        self.errors = errors
        self.results: list[dict] = []

    def present_document(self, doc_id: str, ai_output: dict, actual_error: SeededError | None) -> dict:
        start_time = time.monotonic()

        # In a real system: render doc_id + ai_output in the review UI
        # Here we simulate a participant decision
        decision = self._simulate_participant_review(ai_output, actual_error)

        elapsed = time.monotonic() - start_time

        result = {
            "document_id": doc_id,
            "has_error": actual_error is not None,
            "error_severity": actual_error.severity.value if actual_error else None,
            "participant_caught_error": decision["caught"],
            "participant_decision": decision["action"],
            "time_seconds": round(elapsed, 2),
        }
        self.results.append(result)
        return result

    def _simulate_participant_review(self, ai_output: dict, error: SeededError | None) -> dict:
        # Placeholder: in a real tool this captures UI interactions
        caught = False
        if error:
            # Simulate higher catch rate for blocker-severity errors
            catch_prob = {
                ErrorSeverity.BLOCKER: 0.70,
                ErrorSeverity.HIGH: 0.50,
                ErrorSeverity.MEDIUM: 0.30,
                ErrorSeverity.LOW: 0.15,
            }.get(error.severity, 0.30)
            caught = random.random() < catch_prob
        return {"caught": caught, "action": "override" if caught else "accept"}

    def compute_metrics(self) -> dict:
        total_errors = sum(1 for r in self.results if r["has_error"])
        caught = sum(1 for r in self.results if r["has_error"] and r["participant_caught_error"])
        missed = total_errors - caught

        by_severity: dict[str, dict] = {}
        for r in self.results:
            if r["has_error"]:
                sev = r["error_severity"]
                by_severity.setdefault(sev, {"total": 0, "caught": 0})
                by_severity[sev]["total"] += 1
                if r["participant_caught_error"]:
                    by_severity[sev]["caught"] += 1

        return {
            "participant_id": self.participant_id,
            "total_errors_injected": total_errors,
            "errors_caught": caught,
            "errors_missed": missed,
            "automation_bias_score": round(missed / total_errors, 3) if total_errors else 0,
            "by_severity": by_severity,
            "mean_review_time_seconds": round(
                sum(r["time_seconds"] for r in self.results) / len(self.results), 2
            ) if self.results else 0,
        }
Interpreting Calibration Results by Role
Role-Stratified Analysis

Calibration gaps vary systematically by role, and the variation is informative. In professional services AI deployments, senior practitioners frequently exhibit under-trust because their expertise makes them confident they can do the work without AI. Junior practitioners frequently exhibit over-trust because they have less domain experience to recognise when AI output is wrong. Compliance and legal roles frequently exhibit under-trust because their professional liability frameworks train them toward caution with any automated output.

Role-stratified analysis determines whether a single onboarding approach can address the full user population or whether role-specific interventions are needed. If senior associates are calibrated but partners are under-trust, a single onboarding program will be insufficient. The recommended approach: design a shared foundation and role-specific calibration modules that address the specific gap pattern observed in research.
Compute calibration gap mean and standard deviation per role — high variance within a role may indicate subgroup differences (e.g. by tenure or specialty)
Cross-tabulate automation bias score with prior AI experience — experienced users are not always better calibrated
Compare override rates between roles — an unusually low override rate in a high-stakes role is a risk signal regardless of stated trust
Track time-on-task by trust zone — over-trusting users are often faster, which confirms they are skipping verification rather than improving efficiency
Connecting Research Findings to UX and Engineering Requirements

Trust calibration research is an input to product design, not just an evaluation activity. Each trust zone has a corresponding class of design interventions, and each intervention translates to a UI component or system prompt constraint. This mapping prevents the common outcome where research findings are documented but not actioned: every finding should have an owner in engineering or UX before the research report is distributed.
Finding
Design Intervention
Engineering Requirement
Over-trust: high automation bias on false negatives
Require explicit verification tap for high-stakes findings
Button state: disabled until user expands finding details; log verification events
Over-trust: expects higher accuracy than actual
Accuracy disclosure in onboarding with live benchmark dataset
Onboarding module showing real model accuracy on held-out examples
Under-trust: never uses AI suggestion
Transparency features: source highlighting, reasoning summary
UI: expandable 'Why AI flagged this' panel; link to source sentences
Under-trust: prior bad AI experience
Early-win task design: start with highest-accuracy subtask
Capability gating: surface only the subtask with F1 > 0.90 in first two weeks
Blocker miss rate > 10%
Friction gate on accept for blocker-severity items
Accept button disabled for 5 seconds; requires explicit acknowledgment text
Post-Launch Trust Monitoring
Calibration Does Not Persist

Trust calibration established during research does not persist indefinitely after launch. Model updates change accuracy profiles. New user cohorts join with different prior experiences. Task distributions shift as users discover new ways to use the system. A trust calibration program must include a monitoring phase that tracks behavioural signals in production and triggers new calibration cycles when the signals shift.

The three most reliable production proxies for trust calibration are override rate, time-on-task for AI-assisted versus manual workflows, and error escape rate. Override rate that falls below the model's error rate is a direct indicator of automation bias in the production population. Time-on-task that is lower for AI-assisted than for manual but accompanied by high error escape rate indicates users are rushing through AI review without genuine verification.
Override rate: track weekly by role and by AI confidence band — a declining override rate combined with stable accuracy is a warning sign
Error escape rate: sample AI-assisted outputs through a quality review process; measure what fraction contain errors that users accepted
Time-on-task: compare pre-AI baseline with post-AI; efficiency gains are valid only if error escape rate does not increase
Re-calibration trigger: run a new seeded error session when override rate changes by more than 15 percentage points over a rolling 30-day window
Onboarding cohort comparison: new users systematically differ from trained users; track their calibration gap for the first 30 days separately
Common Research Mistakes in Trust Calibration Studies
Using Production Accuracy as the Only Benchmark

Production accuracy metrics (precision, recall, F1) measure how the model performs on average across all inputs. Trust calibration requires users to be calibrated to accuracy within the specific task domain they perform — which may differ substantially from the aggregate. A model with 85% overall accuracy may have 60% accuracy on the specific subtask a compliance officer performs most frequently. Research that benchmarks against overall accuracy will systematically miscalibrate the cohorts who work in low-accuracy subtask areas.
Recruiting Only Willing Early Adopters

Participants who volunteer for AI research studies systematically over-represent positive attitudes toward technology. A trust calibration study populated entirely by enthusiastic early adopters will underestimate the under-trust population and potentially miss systemic over-trust in the segment that accounts for most of the eventual user base. Recruit from the full intended user population, stratified by technology attitude if possible.
Stratify recruitment by role, tenure, and self-reported AI enthusiasm — include sceptics explicitly
Run sessions before and after participants have used the system in production — calibration changes with experience
Test calibration at the task level, not the system level — users can be well-calibrated on one capability and poorly calibrated on another in the same product
Separate accuracy estimation from error detection — some users have accurate beliefs but still miss errors (a vigilance issue, not a calibration issue)
Include error types that map to the model's actual failure modes, not hypothetical errors
Key Takeaways
Trust calibration research measures whether users rely on AI at an appropriate rate — it is distinct from usability testing and requires behavioural tasks, not just attitudinal surveys.
The calibration gap (expected accuracy minus actual accuracy) is the primary metric: positive means over-trust, negative means under-trust, and both require different interventions.
Seeded errors are the core instrument: inject deliberate AI mistakes into realistic review tasks and measure which errors participants catch, stratified by severity.
Blocker-severity miss rates above 10% are a launch blocker: they indicate users are accepting AI errors with safety, regulatory, or financial consequences at an unacceptable rate.
Research findings map directly to engineering requirements: each trust zone has corresponding UI interventions and system constraints that must be owned before the research report is distributed.
